args.code:
import json

# Load the full business data from file
business_file = var_functions.query_db:5
with open(business_file, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Extract state information from description field
state_data = []
for business in businesses:
    description = business.get('description', '')
    # Extract state from "Located at ... in City, STATE"
    # Look for pattern " in City, XX" or " in City, XX,"
    if ' in ' in description:
        parts = description.split(' in ')
        if len(parts) > 1:
            location_part = parts[-1]
            # Extract state code (2-letter uppercase)
            # Look for pattern: City, ST or City, ST,
            if ', ' in location_part:
                city_state = location_part.split(', ')
                if len(city_state) > 1:
                    state = city_state[-1].strip()
                    # Clean up state (remove trailing punctuation)
                    if ' ' in state:
                        state = state.split()[0]
                    if len(state) == 2 and state.isupper():
                        state_data.append({
                            'business_id': business['business_id'],
                            'state': state,
                            'review_count': int(business['review_count'])
                        })

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(state_data[:10]))  # Preview first 10

args.code:
import json

# Read the business data from file
business_file_path = var_functions.query_db:24

with open(business_file_path, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Parse state from description
business_states = []
for business in businesses:
    description = business.get('description', '')
    state = None
    
    # Example: "Located at 6901 Phelps Rd in Goleta, CA, this facility..."
    if ' in ' in description:
        # Find state code (2 uppercase letters) after comma
        parts = description.split(',')
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            # After a comma, check for 2-letter state code at start
            if len(part) >= 2 and part[:2].isupper():
                state = part[:2]
                break
            # Or check if whole part is 2 uppercase letters
            if len(part) == 2 and part.isupper():
                state = part
                break
                
    if state:
        business_states.append({
            'business_id': business['business_id'],
            'state': state,
            'review_count': int(business['review_count'])
        })

# Count total reviews by state
from collections import defaultdict
state_reviews = defaultdict(int)
for b in business_states:
    state_reviews[b['state']] += b['review_count']

# Find state with highest reviews
max_state = max(state_reviews.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'state': max_state[0], 'total_reviews': max_state[1], 'business_count_by_state': len([b for b in business_states if b['state'] == max_state[0]])}))

I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:18.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

import json

# Load business data
business_file = var_functions.query_db:24
with open(business_file, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Extract states and sum reviews
def extract_state(description):
    if ' in ' not in description:
        return None
    
    location_part = description.split(' in ')[-1]
    # Split by comma to find state
    parts = location_part.split(',')
    for part in parts:
        part = part.strip()
        # Look for 2-letter state code
        if len(part) >= 2:
            # Check if first 2 chars are uppercase (state code)
            if part[:2].isupper() and part[:2].isalpha():
                return part[:2]
    return None

# Sum reviews by state
state_reviews = {}
state_businesses = {}  # Track business_ids per state

for business in businesses:
    state = extract_state(business.get('description', ''))
    if state:
        review_count = int(business['review_count'])
        state_reviews[state] = state_reviews.get(state, 0) + review_count
        
        if state not in state_businesses:
            state_businesses[state] = []
        state_businesses[state].append(business['business_id'])

# Find state with highest reviews
if state_reviews:
    max_state = max(state_reviews.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
    print('__RESULT__:')
    print(json.dumps({
        'state': max_state[0],
        'total_reviews': max_state[1],
        'business_count': len(state_businesses[max_state[0]]),
        'business_ids': state_businesses[max_state[0]][:5]  # First 5 for preview
    }))
else:
    print('__RESULT__:')
    print(json.dumps({'error': 'No states found'}))

val_args.code:
import json

# Load business data
business_file = var_functions.query_db:24
with open(business_file, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Extract states and sum reviews
def extract_state(description):
    if ' in ' not in description:
        return None
    
    location_part = description.split(' in ')[-1]
    # Split by comma to find state
    parts = location_part.split(',')
    for part in parts:
        part = part.strip()
        # Look for 2-letter state code
        if len(part) >= 2:
            # Check if first 2 chars are uppercase (state code)
            if part[:2].isupper() and part[:2].isalpha():
                return part[:2]
    return None

# Sum reviews by state
state_reviews = {}
state_businesses = {}  # Track business_ids per state

for business in businesses:
    state = extract_state(business.get('description', ''))
    if state:
        review_count = int(business['review_count'])
        state_reviews[state] = state_reviews.get(state, 0) + review_count
        
        if state not in state_businesses:
            state_businesses[state] = []
        state_businesses[state].append(business['business_id'])

# Find state with highest reviews
if state_reviews:
    max_state = max(state_reviews.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
    print('__RESULT__:')
    print(json.dumps({
        'state': max_state[0],
        'total_reviews': max_state[1],
        'business_count': len(state_businesses[max_state[0]]),
        'business_ids': state_businesses[max_state[0]][:5]  # First 5 for preview
    }))
else:
    print('__RESULT__:')
    print(json.dumps({'error': 'No states found'}))

val_args.code:
import json

business_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']
with open(business_file, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Extract state
state_reviews = {}
state_businesses = {}

for business in businesses:
    desc = business.get('description', '')
    if ' in ' in desc:
        location = desc.split(' in ')[-1]
        parts = location.split(',')
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if len(part) >= 2 and part[:2].isupper() and part[:2].isalpha():
                state = part[:2]
                review_count = int(business['review_count'])
                state_reviews[state] = state_reviews.get(state, 0) + review_count
                
                if state not in state_businesses:
                    state_businesses[state] = []
                state_businesses[state].append(business['business_id'])
                break

# Find max state
max_state = max(state_reviews.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
result = {
    'state': max_state[0],
    'total_reviews': max_state[1],
    'business_count': len(state_businesses[max_state[0]])
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:18.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json

# Get the business data
business_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']

# If it's a string (file path), load it; if it's already a list, use it directly
if isinstance(business_data, str):
    with open(business_data, 'r') as f:
        businesses = json.load(f)
else:
    businesses = business_data

# Process to find state with most reviews
state_totals = {}
state_business_ids = {}

for business in businesses:
    desc = business.get('description', '')
    
    # Extract state: look for pattern "City, ST"
    if ' in ' in desc:
        location = desc.split(' in ')[-1]
        # Further split by comma
        parts = location.split(',')
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            # Check for 2-letter state code
            if len(part) >= 2 and part[:2].isupper():
                state = part[:2]
                review_count = int(business['review_count'])
                
                # Add to totals
                state_totals[state] = state_totals.get(state, 0) + review_count
                
                # Track business IDs
                if state not in state_business_ids:
                    state_business_ids[state] = []
                state_business_ids[state].append(business['business_id'])
                break

# Find state with maximum reviews
max_state = max(state_totals.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])

result = {
    'state': max_state[0],
    'total_reviews': max_state[1],
    'business_count': len(state_business_ids[max_state[0]])
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

args.code:
import json

# Get business data
business_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:40']
with open(business_file, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Get review data
review_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:42']

# Create business_id -> state mapping
business_state = {}
for business in businesses:
    desc = business.get('description', '')
    state = None
    
    if ' in ' in desc:
        # Extract state from "City, ST" pattern
        location_part = desc.split(' in ')[-1]
        parts = location_part.split(',')
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if len(part) >= 2 and part[:2].isupper() and part[:2].isalpha():
                state = part[:2]
                break
    
    if state:
        business_state[business['business_id']] = state

# Process reviews: map business_ref to state and aggregate
from collections import defaultdict

state_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_reviews': 0, 'total_rating': 0.0, 'businesses': set()})

for review in review_data:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    # Convert businessref_X to businessid_X
    business_id = 'businessid_' + business_ref.split('_')[-1]
    
    if business_id in business_state:
        state = business_state[business_id]
        rating = float(review['avg_rating'])
        review_count = int(review['review_count'])
        
        state_stats[state]['total_reviews'] += review_count
        state_stats[state]['total_rating'] += rating * review_count
        state_stats[state]['businesses'].add(business_id)

# Calculate average rating per state
state_results = {}
for state, stats in state_stats.items():
    if stats['total_reviews'] > 0:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / stats['total_reviews']
        state_results[state] = {
            'total_reviews': stats['total_reviews'],
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'unique_businesses': len(stats['businesses'])
        }

# Find state with highest number of reviews
max_state = max(state_results.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]['total_reviews'])

result = {
    'state': max_state[0],
    'total_reviews': max_state[1]['total_reviews'],
    'avg_rating': max_state[1]['avg_rating'],
    'unique_businesses': max_state[1]['unique_businesses']
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:18.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

val_args.code:
import json

# Get business data
business_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:40']
with open(business_file, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Get review data
review_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:42']

# Create business_id -> state mapping
business_state = {}
for business in businesses:
    desc = business.get('description', '')
    state = None
    
    if ' in ' in desc:
        # Extract state from "City, ST" pattern
        location_part = desc.split(' in ')[-1]
        parts = location_part.split(',')
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if len(part) >= 2 and part[:2].isupper() and part[:2].isalpha():
                state = part[:2]
                break
    
    if state:
        business_state[business['business_id']] = state

# Process reviews: map business_ref to state and aggregate
from collections import defaultdict

state_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_reviews': 0, 'total_rating': 0.0, 'businesses': set()})

for review in review_data:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    # Convert businessref_X to businessid_X
    business_id = 'businessid_' + business_ref.split('_')[-1]
    
    if business_id in business_state:
        state = business_state[business_id]
        rating = float(review['avg_rating'])
        review_count = int(review['review_count'])
        
        state_stats[state]['total_reviews'] += review_count
        state_stats[state]['total_rating'] += rating * review_count
        state_stats[state]['businesses'].add(business_id)

# Calculate average rating per state
state_results = {}
for state, stats in state_stats.items():
    if stats['total_reviews'] > 0:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating'] / stats['total_reviews']
        state_results[state] = {
            'total_reviews': stats['total_reviews'],
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'unique_businesses': len(stats['businesses'])
        }

# Find state with highest number of reviews
max_state = max(state_results.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]['total_reviews'])

result = {
    'state': max_state[0],
    'total_reviews': max_state[1]['total_reviews'],
    'avg_rating': max_state[1]['avg_rating'],
    'unique_businesses': max_state[1]['unique_businesses']
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

args.code:
import json
from collections import defaultdict

# Get business data (stored as list directly)
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:40']

# Get review data (stored as list directly)
review_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:42']

# Create mapping from business_id to state
business_state = {}
for business in businesses:
    desc = business.get('description', '')
    state = None
    
    if ' in ' in desc:
        # Look for state pattern after " in "
        location_part = desc.split(' in ')[-1]
        # Split by comma and look for 2-letter uppercase state code
        parts = location_part.split(',')
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if len(part) >= 2 and part[:2].isupper() and part[:2].isalpha():
                state = part[:2]
                break
    
    if state:
        business_state[business['business_id']] = state

# Aggregate review data by state
state_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_reviews': 0, 'total_rating_weighted': 0.0, 'businesses': set()})

for review in review_data:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    # Convert businessref_X to businessid_X
    business_id = 'businessid_' + business_ref.split('_')[-1]
    
    if business_id in business_state:
        state = business_state[business_id]
        review_count = int(review['review_count'])
        avg_rating = float(review['avg_rating'])
        
        state_stats[state]['total_reviews'] += review_count
        state_stats[state]['total_rating_weighted'] += avg_rating * review_count
        state_stats[state]['businesses'].add(business_id)

# Calculate final results
results = {}
for state, stats in state_stats.items():
    if stats['total_reviews'] > 0:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating_weighted'] / stats['total_reviews']
        results[state] = {
            'total_reviews': stats['total_reviews'],
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'unique_businesses': len(stats['businesses'])
        }

# Find state with highest total reviews
max_state = max(results.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]['total_reviews'])

output = {
    'state': max_state[0],
    'total_reviews': max_state[1]['total_reviews'],
    'avg_rating': max_state[1]['avg_rating'],
    'business_count': max_state[1]['unique_businesses']
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

val_args.code:
import json
from collections import defaultdict

# Get business data (stored as list directly)
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:40']

# Get review data (stored as list directly)
review_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:42']

# Create mapping from business_id to state
business_state = {}
for business in businesses:
    desc = business.get('description', '')
    state = None
    
    if ' in ' in desc:
        # Look for state pattern after " in "
        location_part = desc.split(' in ')[-1]
        # Split by comma and look for 2-letter uppercase state code
        parts = location_part.split(',')
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if len(part) >= 2 and part[:2].isupper() and part[:2].isalpha():
                state = part[:2]
                break
    
    if state:
        business_state[business['business_id']] = state

# Aggregate review data by state
state_stats = defaultdict(lambda: {'total_reviews': 0, 'total_rating_weighted': 0.0, 'businesses': set()})

for review in review_data:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    # Convert businessref_X to businessid_X
    business_id = 'businessid_' + business_ref.split('_')[-1]
    
    if business_id in business_state:
        state = business_state[business_id]
        review_count = int(review['review_count'])
        avg_rating = float(review['avg_rating'])
        
        state_stats[state]['total_reviews'] += review_count
        state_stats[state]['total_rating_weighted'] += avg_rating * review_count
        state_stats[state]['businesses'].add(business_id)

# Calculate final results
results = {}
for state, stats in state_stats.items():
    if stats['total_reviews'] > 0:
        avg_rating = stats['total_rating_weighted'] / stats['total_reviews']
        results[state] = {
            'total_reviews': stats['total_reviews'],
            'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
            'unique_businesses': len(stats['businesses'])
        }

# Find state with highest total reviews
max_state = max(results.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]['total_reviews'])

output = {
    'state': max_state[0],
    'total_reviews': max_state[1]['total_reviews'],
    'avg_rating': max_state[1]['avg_rating'],
    'business_count': max_state[1]['unique_businesses']
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(output))

var_functions.query_db:18.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!
